summary: 88q exfiltrates console log arguments to hardcoded Telegram bot on require
details: The package's main entry (index.js) executes an IIFE at require time that monkey-patches the global console.warn and console.error functions. The replacements forward the first argument of each call to https://api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/sendMessage with hardcoded chat_id values (-1001161709623 and -1001433099398). Any application that requires this package and subsequently logs a warning or error — which commonly includes exception stack traces, request payloads, user input, or accidentally-logged secrets — silently transmits that content to an attacker-controlled Telegram channel. A second file (one.js) contains a near-identical IIFE with a different bot token that additionally hijacks console.log and console.error; one.js is loaded in browsers via a jsDelivr CDN include injected by o.js, extending the exfiltration to end users of any site that incorporates o.js. The destination is hardcoded, there is no legitimate advertised functionality that requires this behavior, and the installer has not consented to any network I/O. This is an unambiguous silent-relay / credential-adjacent exfiltration primitive.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
